title: Quantum Fisher Information under decoherence with explicit wavefunctions
authors: Francesco Musso, Vittorio Vitale, Sara Murciano
abstract: We present a method to estimate the quantum Fisher information (QFI) of many-body quantum states in the presence of decoherence, where its direct evaluation requires the full spectral resolution of the density matrix. We show that, for many-body wave functions known analytically in the occupation-number basis, systematic lower bounds to the QFI can be mapped onto expectation values over a classical probability distribution defined by the wave function amplitudes. This mapping enables efficient estimation via Markov-chain Monte Carlo sampling, with a computational cost that scales as a `slow' exponential ($e^{b L}$ with $b \lesssim 0.6$) and remains manageable for system sizes well beyond exact diagonalization. We specify this framework to Jastrow-Gutzwiller wave functions. We characterize their metrological content by identifying the observables that maximize the QFI and the corresponding scaling with $L$. Then, we analyze the QFI under three physically motivated noise channels: local dephasing, local amplitude damping, and global depolarizing. We compare polynomial and Krylov-based lower bounds across these channels, relating their behavior to the effective rank of the noisy density matrix and to the structure of the operator generating the parameter encoding. The framework extends naturally to other analytically known wave functions and to a broader class of information-theoretic quantities beyond the QFI.
summary: This paper introduces a Monte Carlo method to estimate the Quantum Fisher Information of many-body states subject to decoherence. By mapping QFI bounds to classical sampling, the authors enable the study of large-scale Jastrow-Gutzwiller states. The work characterizes how different noise channels affect the metrological scaling of these states across various physical regimes.
main_problem: Efficiently estimating the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for many-body quantum states in the presence of decoherence, where direct evaluation requires full spectral resolution of the density matrix.
main_result: The authors develop an MCMC-based method to estimate QFI lower bounds with a 'slow' exponential computational cost, demonstrating that Jastrow-Gutzwiller wave functions can achieve metrological advantages even under noise.
method: Mapping QFI lower bounds to expectation values over a classical probability distribution, enabling efficient estimation via Markov-chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling.
model_or_system: Jastrow-Gutzwiller (JG) wave functions on a periodic chain of L sites, which interpolate between GHZ-like, Dicke-like, and critical (Luttinger liquid) regimes via a parameter alpha.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), staggered magnetization (Oz), transverse magnetization (Ox), and correlation functions.
important_parameters: System size (L), interaction parameter (alpha), noise strength (p), and noise channels (local dephasing, local amplitude damping, and global depolarizing).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the wave functions are known analytically in the occupation-number basis; computational cost still scales exponentially with system size.
figures_summary: Figures show the convergence of QFI bounds under depolarizing noise, correlation functions in the critical regime, and the exponential growth of required MCMC samples with system size.
paper_structure: Introduction to QFI and the problem of decoherence; presentation of the MCMC mapping for JG wave functions; analysis of metrological scaling in different alpha regimes; evaluation of various noise channels; discussion of bound accuracy and computational complexity.
why_interesting: It provides a scalable numerical framework for studying the metrological utility of many-body states under realistic noise, bridging the gap between pure-state theory and open-system many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Numerical results on the bounds to the QFI for the dephased JG wave function for different values of α, corresponding to different regimes of the wave function, with local dephasing probability p = 0.05. The operators considered for the different values of α are the ones reported in Table I. The dashed line represents the SQL, i.e., the threshold that signals the presence of entanglement in the state. The solid line corresponds to the exact QFI, obtained via exact diagonalization for small system sizes. For α = 1 we also include the results for the values of the real QFI, by numerically computing the variance of the operator and get the QFI using Eq. 20 (the results for L > 30 are...

FIG. 2. Numerical results on the bounds to the QFI for the damped JG wave function for different values of α, corresponding to different regimes of the wave function with decoherence strength p = 0.05. The operator considered for the different values of α are the ones reported in Table I. The dashed line represents the SQL, i.e., the threshold that signals the presence of entanglement in the state. The solid line corresponds to the exact QFI, obtained via exact diagonalization for small system sizes. For α = 10, B1 immediately saturates to the QFI (solid line) because the operator OZ is diagonal in the occupation basis and effectively reduces it to a low-rank state in the computation of the...

FIG. 3. Numerical results for the bounds on the QFI of the depolarized JG wave function for α = 1, 10. For each value of α, we consider the operator reported in Table I, using the corresponding symbol. Here p = 0.05 denotes the strength of the depolarizing channel.

FIG. 4. Correlation function of the JG wave functions in the critical regime. For α = 1, 3 we computed the correlation functions of Z and X for every L and fitted the results with expected behavior from Eq. A1 to check the agreement between the value of α use to construct the wave function and the one coming from the fit. In the left column the value of α coming from the fit is plotted against the system size, while on the right we report the values of the correlations at L = 50 with the corresponding fits.

FIG. 5. Number of the Markov chain samples of the JG distribution, for different values of α, required to get the total variation distance under the 0.1 threshold. The dashed lines represent an exponential fit aebL on the data. For α = 3, we get a = 32, b = 0.374, and for α = 1, we get a = 9, b = 0.575.
